skill.name: azure-ai-ml-py
skill.description: Reference guide for Azure Machine Learning SDK v2 in Python, covering clients, workspaces, assets, compute, jobs, pipelines, environments, and datastores.
skill.summary: Use Azure Machine Learning SDK v2 patterns for Python ML workspace and job workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are Markdown code fences, inline backticks, and description text rather than executable skill logic. The skill is a documentation-only Azure ML SDK reference with low residual risk from examples that read Azure identifiers from environment variables and show cloud resource creation commands.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command detections point to Markdown code fences and inline backticks in SKILL.md, not Ruby backtick execution or executable skill code. The only shell command shown is a package installation example, and no dynamic command construction or automatic execution is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown documentation, and the flagged lines are code block delimiters or inline formatting. No Ruby code or runtime command execution path exists in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Read Azure Resource Identifiers
low_findings.description: The skill shows os.environ reads for AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID, AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP, and AZURE_ML_WORKSPACE_NAME. These are configuration identifiers for Azure ML client setup, not credential exfiltration, but users should avoid exposing sensitive tenant or subscription details in shared logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable names and surrounding MLClient initialization make the configuration purpose clear. No network transmission, logging, or secret collection behavior is shown beyond normal Azure SDK authentication setup.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are Description Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections occur on ordinary description strings, including the skill description and resource descriptions. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithm usage in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain prose fields named description or harmless resource descriptions. There is no cryptographic API call, hashing code, or cipher selection in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Azure Resource Creation Examples Can Affect Cloud Accounts
low_findings.description: The examples show creating or updating Azure ML workspaces, data assets, models, compute, jobs, pipelines, and environments. This is expected for an Azure ML SDK skill, but users should review cost, quota, and permission impact before running examples against a real subscription.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The operations are explicit Azure SDK examples with legitimate purpose. The concern is operational impact rather than malicious behavior because no hidden execution or unauthorized endpoint is present.

content.user_title: Build Azure ML Workflows in Python
content.value_statement: Azure ML SDK v2 has many resource types and job patterns to remember. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concise examples for common Python MLOps tasks.
actual_capabilities: Shows MLClient setup with DefaultAzureCredential and environment-based workspace identifiers.
Provides examples for creating and listing Azure ML workspaces, compute clusters, and datastores.
Explains data asset and model registration with Azure ML SDK v2 entities.
Demonstrates command jobs, job log streaming, and simple pipeline submission patterns.
Lists common MLClient operation groups for workspaces, jobs, models, data, compute, and environments.
limitations: Does not authenticate users or manage Azure credentials directly.
Does not include complete runnable projects, training scripts, or component definitions.
Examples may create Azure resources and costs if copied into a live subscription.
Does not cover deployment endpoints, monitoring, or advanced governance workflows.

use_cases.title: Set up an Azure ML workspace client
use_cases.description: Use the skill to recall the imports, credential pattern, and workspace identifiers needed for MLClient.

use_cases.title: Register datasets and models
use_cases.description: Use the examples to create consistent Azure ML data assets and model registry entries.

use_cases.title: Draft training jobs and pipelines
use_cases.description: Use the command job and pipeline patterns as a starting point for repeatable training workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create an MLClient
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an existing Azure ML workspace.
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the azure-ai-ml-py skill, help me initialize an Azure ML MLClient in Python with DefaultAzureCredential and environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Register training data
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare a data asset for a training workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the azure-ai-ml-py skill, outline the steps to register a URI folder as an Azure ML data asset with a version and description.

prompt_templates.title: Submit a training job
prompt_templates.scenario: Run a Python training script on Azure ML compute.
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the azure-ai-ml-py skill, draft an Azure ML SDK v2 command job for a Python training script with data and learning-rate inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a pipeline workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with multiple training stages.
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the azure-ai-ml-py skill, help me design an Azure ML pipeline that prepares data, trains a model, and returns a registered model output.

output_examples.input: I need to connect to my Azure ML workspace from Python.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains the MLClient setup, required Azure identifiers, and DefaultAzureCredential flow in plain steps.

output_examples.input: I want to register a model after training.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes the model entity fields, versioning approach, and create-or-update workflow without exposing credentials.

output_examples.input: I need a repeatable training job on Azure ML compute.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes the command job structure, input binding, environment selection, compute target, and log streaming step.

best_practices: Use explicit versions for data assets, models, and environments before submitting production jobs.
Set compute scale-down values and review Azure costs before creating clusters or running jobs.
Keep subscription, resource group, and workspace values in local configuration instead of hardcoding them.
anti_patterns: Do not copy examples into production without checking Azure permissions, quotas, and naming rules.
Do not store Azure credentials, tokens, or secrets directly in prompts or source files.
Do not use unversioned assets for training workflows that must be reproducible.

faq.question: What Azure package does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers azure-ai-ml, the Azure Machine Learning SDK v2 package for Python.

faq.question: Can this skill create Azure resources by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference examples. Resource creation happens only if a user runs adapted code with Azure credentials.

faq.question: Does it include authentication guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows DefaultAzureCredential and environment-based workspace identifiers for MLClient setup.

faq.question: Does it cover model deployment endpoints?
faq.answer: No. The current content focuses on workspaces, assets, compute, jobs, pipelines, environments, and datastores.

faq.question: Is it suitable for Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides compact SDK patterns that Claude Code and Codex can apply while editing Python ML workflows.

faq.question: What should users verify before running examples?
faq.answer: Users should verify Azure subscription scope, resource group, workspace name, compute cost, and required permissions.
